question: What is the fundamental group $\pi_1$ and what does it detect?

options: The group of homotopy classes of loops based at a point, with concatenation as the operation; it detects 1-dimensional holes. Trivial $\pi_1$ = simply connected; e.g. $\pi_1(S^1) = \mathbb Z$, $\pi_1(S^2) = 0$, $\pi_1(T^2) = \mathbb Z^2$, $\pi_1(SO(3)) = \mathbb Z_2$
The group of all continuous functions on the space (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (this describes a different phenomenon)
A number equal to the dimension of the space (this reverses the actual relationship) (a common misconception) (this conflates cause and effect)
Always trivial for every space (not supported by the evidence) (an outdated view superseded by later work) (this confuses two distinct concepts)
passive.explanation: The fundamental group is the first and most intuitive algebraic invariant: loops that can be continuously shrunk to a point are trivial, and $\pi_1$ records the distinct 'wrappings'. It is a homotopy invariant (homotopy-equivalent spaces share it) but generally non-abelian (higher homotopy groups $\pi_n$ are abelian for $n\ge2$). Physical relevance: $\pi_1$ classifies topological defects (vortices, the $\mathbb Z_2$ of $SO(3)$ explaining spinor sign changes), the Aharonov-Bohm effect ($\pi_1$ of the punctured plane $= \mathbb Z$), and anyon braiding lives in $\pi_1$ of configuration space (the braid group in 2D).

active.prompt: Match each algebraic-topology invariant with its role:

pairs.definition: Loops up to homotopy; detects 1D holes; classifies covering spaces and line defects
Cycles modulo boundaries; counts $n$-dimensional holes; computable, abelian
$\sum(-1)^n b_n = V-E+F$; topological invariant; Gauss-Bonnet links it to curvature
Maps of $S^n$ up to homotopy; e.g. $\pi_3(S^2)=\mathbb Z$ (Hopf); classifies textures/skyrmions

active.explanation: Algebraic topology converts topological questions into algebra. Homotopy groups ($\pi_n$) are powerful but hard to compute; homology ($H_n$) and cohomology are computable and abelian. Betti numbers $b_n = \mathrm{rank}\,H_n$ count holes of each dimension; their alternating sum is the Euler characteristic, the most robust single invariant.

question: What is homology and what does it measure?

options: Homology groups $H_n = \ker\partial_n / \operatorname{im}\partial_{n+1}$ (cycles modulo boundaries) count $n$-dimensional holes; $b_n = \mathrm{rank}\,H_n$ are the Betti numbers — $b_0$ = connected components, $b_1$ = independent loops, $b_2$ = enclosed voids
Homology counts the number of points in a space (a common misconception) (not supported by the evidence) (true only as a special case, not in general)
It is the same as the fundamental group in all degrees (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (this confuses two distinct concepts)
Homology is defined only for graphs (this describes a different phenomenon) (this reverses the actual relationship) (an outdated view superseded by later work)
passive.explanation: Build a space from simplices (or cells); the boundary operator $\partial$ satisfies $\partial^2 = 0$, so boundaries are cycles. Homology measures cycles that are not boundaries — genuine holes. For a torus: $b_0=1$ (connected), $b_1=2$ (two independent loops), $b_2=1$ (encloses a void), giving $\chi = 1-2+1 = 0$. Homology is functorial, computable (via the boundary matrices / Smith normal form), and abelian, making it more tractable than homotopy. It is dual (via the universal coefficient theorem and Poincare duality) to cohomology, and the de Rham theorem identifies real cohomology with differential forms. Persistent homology extends these ideas to data analysis (topological data analysis).

question: What is the Euler characteristic and why is it so robust?

options: $\chi = \sum_n (-1)^n b_n = V - E + F$ for a polyhedral decomposition; it is a homotopy invariant independent of the triangulation, equals $2-2g$ for a genus-$g$ surface, and connects to curvature (Gauss-Bonnet) and to vector fields (Poincare-Hopf)
It depends on how finely you triangulate the space (this reverses the actual relationship) (this conflates cause and effect) (an outdated view superseded by later work)
It is only defined for convex polyhedra (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (a common misconception) (true only as a special case, not in general)
It always equals the dimension (this confuses two distinct concepts) (this describes a different phenomenon) (inconsistent with standard results in the field)
passive.explanation: Euler's formula $V-E+F = 2$ for convex polyhedra generalises to $\chi = \sum(-1)^n(\text{number of }n\text{-cells}) = \sum(-1)^n b_n$ — manifestly the same whether computed combinatorially or from Betti numbers, hence triangulation-independent. For closed surfaces $\chi = 2-2g$ (sphere 2, torus 0, double torus $-2$). Deep links: Gauss-Bonnet ($\int K\,dA = 2\pi\chi$) ties it to geometry; the Poincare-Hopf theorem equates $\chi$ to the sum of indices of any vector field's zeros (so a nonzero $\chi$ forbids a nowhere-zero field — the 'hairy ball theorem' for $S^2$, $\chi=2$). Its robustness makes it a touchstone invariant across geometry, topology, and physics.

question: What are higher homotopy groups and how do they classify topological textures?

options: $\pi_n(X)$ = homotopy classes of maps $S^n \to X$; in physics, field configurations on compactified space are maps into the order-parameter (vacuum) manifold, and $\pi_n$ classifies solitons/defects — e.g. $\pi_2$ for skyrmions/monopoles, $\pi_3$ for instantons, $\pi_1$ for vortices
Higher homotopy groups are always trivial (true only as a special case, not in general) (this conflates cause and effect) (inconsistent with standard results in the field)
They classify only the number of points (not supported by the evidence) (this reverses the actual relationship) (an outdated view superseded by later work)
$\pi_n$ depends on a choice of metric (this describes a different phenomenon) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (a common misconception)
passive.explanation: After symmetry breaking, the vacuum manifold $M$ (degenerate ground states) determines topological defects via homotopy: $\pi_0(M)$ = domain walls, $\pi_1(M)$ = line defects (vortices, cosmic strings), $\pi_2(M)$ = point defects (monopoles, 2D skyrmions), $\pi_3(M)$ = textures/instantons. Examples: superfluid $^4$He vortices ($\pi_1(U(1)) = \mathbb Z$); 't Hooft-Polyakov monopoles ($\pi_2(S^2)=\mathbb Z$); Yang-Mills instantons ($\pi_3(SU(2)) = \mathbb Z$); magnetic skyrmions in chiral magnets ($\pi_2 = \mathbb Z$, technologically promising for memory). The famous $\pi_3(S^2) = \mathbb Z$ (Hopf fibration) classifies Hopfions. This homotopy classification of defects (Kibble-Mermin) is a cornerstone of condensed matter and cosmology.

question: What does Poincare duality state for closed oriented manifolds?

options: On a closed oriented $n$-manifold, $H^k(M) \cong H_{n-k}(M)$ (and $H^k \cong H^{n-k}$ over a field), so Betti numbers satisfy $b_k = b_{n-k}$; intersection of complementary-dimension cycles realises the pairing
All cohomology groups are isomorphic to each other (true only as a special case, not in general) (this describes a different phenomenon)
It holds only for spheres (this confuses two distinct concepts) (this conflates cause and effect) (inconsistent with standard results in the field)
It states $H^k = 0$ for all $k$ (an outdated view superseded by later work) (a common misconception) (this reverses the actual relationship)
passive.explanation: Poincare duality is a fundamental symmetry of manifold topology: capping with the fundamental class gives an isomorphism between $k$-cohomology and $(n-k)$-homology. Consequences: Betti numbers are palindromic ($b_k = b_{n-k}$), and there is a nondegenerate intersection (cup-product) pairing $H^k\times H^{n-k}\to\mathbb R$ — geometrically, how cycles of complementary dimension intersect. For surfaces this is the intersection form on loops; for 4-manifolds the intersection form on $H^2$ is a powerful invariant (Donaldson, Freedman). Variants: Lefschetz duality (manifolds with boundary), Serre duality (complex geometry), and Verdier duality. It encodes the deep self-referential structure of manifolds and is essential to index theory and gauge-theoretic invariants.

question: What is the Poincare conjecture and how was it resolved?

options: Every simply-connected closed 3-manifold is homeomorphic to $S^3$; proved by Perelman (2003) using Hamilton's Ricci flow with surgery, controlling singularities to deform any such manifold to the round sphere — completing the geometrisation of 3-manifolds
It was disproved by a counterexample in 1980 (a common misconception) (this confuses two distinct concepts) (inconsistent with standard results in the field)
It concerns only 2-dimensional surfaces (an outdated view superseded by later work) (this reverses the actual relationship) (this describes a different phenomenon)
It remains an open problem today (not supported by the evidence) (this conflates cause and effect) (true only as a special case, not in general)
passive.explanation: Poincare (1904) asked whether a closed 3-manifold with trivial fundamental group must be the 3-sphere. The higher-dimensional analogues fell first (Smale $n\ge5$ via the h-cobordism theorem, Freedman $n=4$ topologically), leaving dimension 3. Perelman (2002-2003) proved it — and the broader Thurston geometrisation conjecture — using Hamilton's Ricci flow $\partial_t g = -2\,\mathrm{Ric}$, an analytic 'heat equation for the metric' that smooths geometry, combined with surgery to excise developing singularities and monotonicity functionals (entropy) to control the flow. It is the only solved Clay Millennium Prize problem (Perelman declined both the prize and the Fields Medal). The proof fused geometry, analysis (PDE), and topology — a landmark of modern mathematics.
